schools.summary: Paul's charge to masters (completing the household duties begun in ch. 3): 'give unto your servants (slaves) that which is just (righteous, fair) and equal (equitable, fair-dealing)' — masters owe their servants justice and fairness (fair provision, treatment, and reward), not arbitrary or harsh treatment, the master bound by justice no less than the servant by obedience (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Henry: masters are to remember that 'ye also have a Master (Lord) in heaven' — they too are servants, accountable to God, who will judge their treatment of those under them; this checks all tyranny and harshness.

themes: Masters, give unto your servants that which is just and equal
knowing that ye also have a Master in heaven
the charge to masters to deal justly
accountable to a Master in heaven

schools.summary: Paul's charge: 'continue (proskartereite — persevere, be steadfast, devote yourselves constantly) in prayer' — believers are to be constant and persistent in prayer, never giving it up; 'and watch (be watchful, alert, vigilant) in the same' — prayer accompanied by watchfulness, an attentive, wakeful spirit, not drowsy or careless; 'with thanksgiving' — gratitude for mercies received mingling with every petition (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Continue in prayer, and watch in the same with thanksgiving
the call to persevere in watchful prayer
constant, vigilant prayer
thanksgiving joined to prayer

schools.summary: Paul asks their intercession for himself and his fellow-workers: 'praying also for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance (thyran tou logou — a door for the word, an opportunity to preach)' — that God would give opportunity and freedom to proclaim the gospel; 'to speak the mystery of Christ' — the once-hidden, now-revealed truth of Christ and the gospel, especially the inclusion of the Gentiles (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Wesley: it is 'for which (the mystery, the gospel) I am also in bonds' — Paul's very imprisonment is for preaching this mystery, yet even in bonds he seeks opportunity to proclaim it.

themes: praying also for us, that God would open unto us a door of utterance, to speak the mystery of Christ
for which I am also in bonds
Paul's request for prayer for an open door
longing to preach the mystery of Christ

schools.summary: The purpose of the open door (v.3): 'that I may make it (the mystery of Christ) manifest (clear, plain, openly known)' — Paul's desire is not merely to speak but to make the gospel clearly understood; 'as I ought (as it is my duty, as is fitting) to speak' — he feels bound to declare the gospel plainly and faithfully, asking prayer that he may discharge this duty as he should (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: even the great apostle asks prayer that he may speak rightly — for the ability to make the gospel manifest is God's gift, not his own attainment; he leans on the prayers of the church.

themes: That I may make it manifest, as I ought to speak
Paul's desire to make the gospel plain
declaring the mystery as he ought
leaning on the prayers of the church

schools.summary: Paul's charge concerning conduct before the unconverted: 'walk (live, conduct yourselves) in wisdom toward them that are without (those outside the church, the unbelievers)' — believers are to behave wisely and prudently in the sight of those not yet in the faith; 'redeeming the time (buying up the opportunity, making the most of every occasion)' — seizing every opportunity, especially to do good and commend the gospel to outsiders (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): the conduct of believers toward outsiders is a witness — wise, blameless behaviour adorns the gospel and may win them, while folly or scandal repels; much depends on how the church appears to the world.

themes: Walk in wisdom toward them that are without, redeeming the time
the call to walk wisely toward outsiders
conduct a witness to the world
redeeming the time

schools.summary: Paul's rule for the tongue: 'let your speech (conversation) be alway (at all times) with grace (gracious, pleasant, winning, full of spiritual benefit)' — the believer's speech always kind, edifying, and gracious; 'seasoned with salt' — as salt gives savour and preserves from corruption, so speech is to be wholesome, savoury, and preserving, not insipid, not corrupt, but with point and wholesomeness (some adding the sense of wit and good sense); 'that ye may know how ye ought to answer every man' — equipped to give a fitting, wise reply to each (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: gracious, savoury speech equips the believer to know how he ought to answer every man — to give a fitting, wise reply to each person, whatever his question or need.

themes: Let your speech be alway with grace, seasoned with salt
that ye may know how ye ought to answer every man
the call to gracious, well-seasoned speech
speech savoury like salt

schools.summary: Paul introduces the bearer of the letter: 'all my state (my affairs, how I fare) shall Tychicus declare (make known) unto you' — Tychicus would tell them in person all about Paul's circumstances; the threefold commendation 'a beloved brother (dear in the faith), and a faithful minister (diakonos — a trusty servant of the gospel) and fellowservant (syndoulos — a fellow-slave of Christ) in the Lord' — high praise of a trusted companion (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

Tertullian: such faithful ministers and fellow-servants were the channels through whom the apostle's word and care reached the churches; Tychicus bears both the letter and Paul's heart to the Colossians.

themes: All my state shall Tychicus declare unto you
a beloved brother, and a faithful minister and fellowservant in the Lord
Paul commending Tychicus
the trusted messenger

schools.summary: The double purpose of Tychicus's mission: 'whom I have sent unto you for the same purpose, that he might know your estate (learn your circumstances, how you fare)' — to bring back word of the Colossians' condition to Paul; 'and comfort (encourage, console, strengthen) your hearts' — Tychicus was to bring them encouragement, cheering them with news of Paul and with his own ministry; the mission serving both ends, binding apostle and church together in mutual concern (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: whom I have sent unto you... that he might know your estate, and comfort your hearts
Tychicus sent to learn their estate and comfort their hearts
the mutual care of apostle and church
the comforting errand

schools.summary: Tychicus is accompanied by 'Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother' — the same Onesimus who was the runaway slave of Philemon, now converted and sent back, commended by Paul as a true brother (cf. the Epistle to Philemon); 'who is one of you' — a Colossian, belonging to their own community, now received as one of their own though once a fugitive slave; the two together 'shall make known unto you all things which are done here,' reporting Paul's situation at Rome (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: With Onesimus, a faithful and beloved brother, who is one of you
They shall make known unto you all things which are done here
Onesimus now a faithful brother
the transforming grace of the gospel

schools.summary: The first to send greeting: 'Aristarchus my fellowprisoner (synaichmalotos — fellow-captive)' — a companion of Paul (Acts 19:29; 27:2) who shared his imprisonment; 'and Marcus (John Mark), sister's son (cousin) to Barnabas' — the same Mark who had once departed from Paul (Acts 15:37-39), now reconciled and again a fellow-worker; 'touching whom ye received commandments: if he come unto you, receive him' — Paul instructs them to welcome Mark, a full restoration of one who had once been the occasion of a sharp parting (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Aristarchus my fellowprisoner saluteth you, and Marcus, sister's son to Barnabas
if he come unto you, receive him
the greetings of Aristarchus and Marcus
Mark restored and commended

schools.summary: The third of this group: 'Jesus, which is called Justus' — otherwise unknown, bearing the common Jewish name Jesus (Joshua) with the Latin surname Justus; these three (Aristarchus, Mark, Justus) 'are of the circumcision' — Jewish believers, of whom only these few stood with Paul as fellow-workers, the rest largely opposing or holding aloof (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: 'these only are my fellowworkers unto the kingdom of God, which have been a comfort (consolation, encouragement) unto me' — amid much opposition, these few faithful Jewish workers were a real comfort to the imprisoned apostle.

themes: Jesus, which is called Justus, who are of the circumcision
these only are my fellowworkers unto the kingdom of God, which have been a comfort unto me
the Jewish fellow-workers who comforted Paul
the faithful few

schools.summary: Epaphras, the founder or evangelist of the Colossian church (1:7): 'who is one of you (a Colossian), a servant (bondservant) of Christ' — their own beloved minister, now with Paul, sending greeting; 'always labouring fervently (agonizomenos — striving, wrestling, agonizing, as in a contest) for you in prayers' — Epaphras prayed with intense, wrestling earnestness, an athlete's struggle in intercession; 'that ye may stand perfect (mature, complete) and complete (fully assured, filled) in all the will of God' (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

Wesley: Epaphras wrestled that they may stand perfect and complete in all the will of God — that they might be established, mature, and fully assured in all that God wills, the goal of his agonizing prayer.

themes: Epaphras, who is one of you, a servant of Christ... always labouring fervently for you in prayers
that ye may stand perfect and complete in all the will of God
Epaphras wrestling in prayer for them
agonizing intercession for their maturity

schools.summary: Paul adds his own witness to Epaphras's labour: 'I bear him record (I testify on his behalf), that he hath a great zeal (polyn ponon — much labour, deep concern, earnest toil) for you' — Paul vouches for the intensity of Epaphras's care; his zeal extending beyond Colosse to the neighbouring churches, 'them that are in Laodicea, and them in Hierapolis' — the three cities of the Lycus valley, all objects of his prayerful concern, his pastoral heart embracing the whole region (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

themes: I bear him record, that he hath a great zeal for you, and them that are in Laodicea, and them in Hierapolis
Paul's witness to the zeal of Epaphras
his wide pastoral care
the three churches of the Lycus valley

schools.summary: The greeting of 'Luke, the beloved physician' — the writer of the Gospel and Acts, Paul's faithful companion and friend, here described by his profession, a beloved fellow-worker who stayed with Paul to the end (2 Timothy 4:11), his medical skill perhaps a comfort to the often-afflicted apostle; 'and Demas' sends greeting too — but his name is given without any commendation, and he would later forsake Paul, 'having loved this present world' (2 Timothy 4:10), the bare mention here standing in poignant contrast to his later defection (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Luke, the beloved physician, and Demas, greet you
the greetings of Luke and Demas
Luke the faithful companion
Demas greeting without commendation

schools.summary: Paul greets 'the brethren which are in Laodicea,' the neighbouring church closely bound to Colosse, and 'Nymphas, and the church which is in his house' — a house-church gathered in the home of a leading believer (before separate church buildings existed, as Romans 16:5; 1 Corinthians 16:19; Philemon 2). There is a textual variety: the name may be masculine 'Nymphas' (with 'his house,' D E K L) or feminine 'Nympha' (with 'her house,' B 67), while the best-attested reading is 'their house' (Aleph A C P, referring to Nymphas and the brethren); Robertson concludes it is not possible to tell whether 'her' or 'his.' Of this person, host of the house-church, nothing more is known.

Calvin: from this one household a rule is laid down for all Christian homes — that they be so many 'little churches,' each trained up in the fear of the Lord under holy discipline, forming in itself the likeness of a church.

debates.question: Is the host of the house-church 'Nymphas' (masculine) or 'Nympha' (feminine)?

sides.position: Indeterminate — the manuscripts vary between 'his house,' 'her house,' and 'their house'; cannot be decided
Masculine 'Nymphas,' with 'the church in his house' (the received text)

themes: Salute the brethren which are in Laodicea, and Nymphas, and the church which is in his house
the greeting to Laodicea and the house-church
the Nymphas/Nympha textual question
the household a little church

schools.summary: Paul directs the public reading and interchange of his letters: 'when this epistle is read among you (publicly, to the assembled church), cause that it be read also in the church of the Laodiceans' — the counsel equally needful for both neighbouring churches, a window into how the apostolic letters were first published and came to be widely received and 'canonised' (as also 1 Thessalonians 5:27). 'The epistle from Laodicea' is most likely our Epistle to the Ephesians — an encyclical (circular) letter to the churches of Asia, one copy reaching Laodicea and to be passed on to Colossae; Marcion expressly called Ephesians the letter 'to the Laodiceans' (Ellicott, Robertson). The extant Latin 'Epistle to the Laodiceans' is rejected as an obvious forgery, a tame compilation of Pauline phrases.

Calvin (with Wesley): 'the epistle from Laodicea' was a letter sent from Laodicea to Paul, the perusal of which would profit the Colossians ('from Laodicea, not to Laodicea,' Wesley) — neighbouring towns having many things in common. Calvin sharply rejects both the notion that Paul wrote it and the extant forgery, a 'gross imposture' by 'some worthless person' who had the audacity to forge so insipid a letter under Paul's name.

sides.position: Our Epistle to the Ephesians — an encyclical letter, called 'to the Laodiceans' by Marcion
A genuine Pauline letter to Laodicea, now lost
A letter sent from Laodicea to Paul, profitable for the Colossians to read
The extant Latin 'Epistle to the Laodiceans' — rejected by all as a forgery

themes: cause that it be read also in the church of the Laodiceans; and that ye likewise read the epistle from Laodicea
the charge to exchange epistles
the crux of the epistle from Laodicea
the extant Latin forgery rejected

schools.summary: Paul's message to a minister of the church: 'say to Archippus (named also in Philemon 2, perhaps Philemon's son), Take heed (blepe — keep an eye on, give attention to) the ministry (diakonia — the office of ministering in the gospel) which thou hast received in the Lord, that thou fulfil (fully discharge, make full proof of) it' — an exhortation to faithful diligence; the charge need not imply that Archippus had been negligent, but rather encourages him to diligence and zeal, fitting for any minister amid false teachers (Barnes, Robertson, Clarke).

Calvin (with Gill): the charge gently stirs up one who may have grown cold, and is notably given through the whole church — that the Colossians themselves urge their pastor on; for ministers, though endowed with authority, are not exempt from being admonished by the flock.

themes: say to Archippus, Take heed to the ministry which thou hast received in the Lord, that thou fulfil it
the charge to Archippus to fulfil his ministry
faithful diligence in the ministry
the charge given through the church

schools.summary: Paul, who dictated the letter, now adds the closing in his own hand: 'the salutation by the hand of me Paul' — his personal signature, the mark of authenticity he gave his letters (2 Thessalonians 3:17), guarding against forgery; 'remember my bonds (my chains, my imprisonment)' — the touching request that they not forget he writes as a prisoner for the gospel, an appeal both for their prayers and sympathy and a seal of his testimony, the very hand that wrote being chained; 'grace be with you. Amen' — the closing benediction invoking the free favour of God on the whole church (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: The salutation by the hand of me Paul. Remember my bonds. Grace be with you. Amen
Paul's closing salutation in his own hand
remember my bonds
the closing benediction of grace
